topic: Peninsular Rivers — East Flowing & West Flowing

questions.text: Which is the longest peninsular river of India?
questions.explanation: The Godavari (~1,465 km) is the longest peninsular river of India. It originates at Trimbakeshwar near Nashik, Maharashtra and flows through Maharashtra, Telangana, and Andhra Pradesh before emptying into the Bay of Bengal near Rajahmundry. It is called 'Dakshina Ganga' (Ganga of the South) because of its length and the large basin it drains. Narmada (~1,312 km) is the longest WEST-flowing peninsular river — a common confusion in SSC.

questions.text: The Hirakud Dam, the longest earthen dam in India, is built across which river?
questions.explanation: Hirakud Dam is built across the Mahanadi River near Sambalpur, Odisha. At ~26 km, it is the longest earthen dam in India and one of the longest in the world. The dam was completed in 1957 and was one of India's first major post-independence irrigation and flood control projects. The Mahanadi originates in Chhattisgarh and the dam is located in Odisha, where the river enters the coastal plains.

questions.text: The Jog Falls (Gerosoppa Falls), the highest plunge waterfall in India, is on which river?
questions.explanation: Jog Falls (also called Gerosoppa or Gersoppa Falls) is on the Sharavathi River in the Shimoga (Shivamogga) district of Karnataka. At 253 m, it is the highest plunge waterfall in India. Do not confuse with Dudhsagar Falls (on Mandovi, Goa-Karnataka border, ~310 m total but segmented) or Shivanasamudra Falls (on Cauvery, Karnataka — famous for India's first hydroelectric station). Mandovi flows through Goa (Panaji is on it).

questions.text: Which city is situated on the banks of the Musi River?
questions.explanation: Hyderabad (Telangana) stands on the Musi River, a tributary of the Krishna River. The Musi flows through the heart of Hyderabad — the historic Charminar and the Hussain Sagar Lake are close to it. Bengaluru is on the Arkavathi (tributary of Cauvery). Do not confuse the two south Indian capitals with their rivers. The 1908 Musi floods devastated Hyderabad, after which the Osman Sagar and Himayat Sagar reservoirs were built to control it.

questions.text: Why do Narmada and Tapti rivers flow westward into the Arabian Sea, while most other peninsular rivers flow eastward?
options: Because the Western Ghats act as a barrier redirecting them west
Because they originate in the Western Ghats, which drain towards the coast
Because they flow through rift valleys (tectonic fault depressions) between uplifted blocks
Because rainfall in the Vindhya region pushes them westward
questions.explanation: Narmada and Tapti are exceptions to the eastward tilt of the Deccan. They flow westward because they follow rift valleys — elongated troughs formed by geological faulting where the land between two fault lines sank (graben). Narmada flows in a rift valley between Vindhya (north) and Satpura (south); Tapti flows in a rift south of Satpura. These fault lines run east-west, and the rivers simply follow the pre-existing depressions westward to the Arabian Sea. This is fundamentally different from how rivers ordinarily form on a tilted surface.

questions.text: India's first hydroelectric power station (1902) was located at Shivanasamudra Falls. Which river forms this waterfall?
questions.explanation: Shivanasamudra Falls on the Cauvery River (Karnataka) was the site of India's first hydroelectric power station (1902). The power was transmitted to the Kolar Gold Fields (KGF) some 145 km away — making it one of the world's earliest long-distance AC power transmissions. The falls are located near Mandya, Karnataka. Do not confuse Shivanasamudra (Cauvery, first hydroelectric) with Jog Falls (Sharavathi, highest plunge waterfall).

questions.text: Which river is the widest waterfall in India located on?
questions.explanation: Chitrakote Falls (also called the 'Indian Niagara') on the Indravati River in Bastar district, Chhattisgarh, is India's widest waterfall — spreading up to 300 m in width during monsoon. The Indravati is a major tributary of the Godavari. Jog Falls (Sharavathi) is the tallest plunge; Chitrakote is the widest. Dhuandhar (Narmada/Jabalpur) is famous for the 'smoke-like' spray at Bhedaghat's marble gorge.

questions.text: The Luni River of Rajasthan is unusual because:
options: It flows through the Deccan Plateau from east to west
It flows into the Rann of Kutch and has no outlet to the sea; its water becomes saline in the lower course
It originates from the Himalayas and is perennial
It forms the largest delta in western India
questions.explanation: The Luni (meaning 'salty' in local language) originates from the Pushkar/Nag Hills near Ajmer in the Aravallis and flows southwest into the Rann of Kutch — a saline marshland. The Luni has no outlet to the Arabian Sea; it is a terminal (inland) drainage river. Crucially, the river's water is fresh in its upper reaches but becomes progressively saline/brackish towards the lower course as it flows through arid zones. It is Rajasthan's only significant river — but this quirky characteristic makes it an SSC favourite.

questions.text: Surat, one of India's historically greatest trading ports, stands on which river?
questions.explanation: Surat (Gujarat) stands on the Tapti (Tapi) River. Surat was the most important port of the Mughal Empire and was where the British East India Company established its first trading post (factory) in India in 1608. The Tapti originates in the Satpura Range near Betul (MP) and flows through Maharashtra and Gujarat before entering the Gulf of Khambhat as an estuary. Ahmedabad stands on Sabarmati (where Gandhi's ashram is); Bharuch stands on Narmada.

questions.text: The Idukki Dam in Kerala is India's highest arch dam. It is built on which river?
questions.explanation: The Idukki Dam — a double-curvature arch dam — is built on the Periyar River in the Idukki district of Kerala. It is India's highest arch dam. The Periyar is also Kerala's longest river (~235 km). Periyar National Park/Tiger Reserve (also called Thekkady) is located in the upper basin of the Periyar. Do not confuse: Bhakra = largest gravity dam (Sutlej, HP); Tehri = tallest dam (Bhagirathi, Uttarakhand); Idukki = highest arch dam (Periyar, Kerala).

questions.text: The Cauvery delta in Tamil Nadu is historically called the 'Granary of South India'. Which city in this delta is famous for the UNESCO-listed Brihadeeswarar Temple built by the Cholas?
questions.explanation: Thanjavur (Tanjore) is located in the heart of the Cauvery delta — the 'Granary of South India' — and is home to the Brihadeeswarar Temple (Big Temple), built by Rajaraja Chola I in 1010 CE. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (part of the Great Living Chola Temples group). Madurai is on the Vaigai River (a separate river south of Cauvery). Trichy is on the Cauvery but upstream of the delta.

questions.text: The Tungabhadra River, a major tributary of the Krishna, is historically significant because:
options: It originates at Talakaveri in Coorg — the same source as the Cauvery
Hampi, the capital of the Vijayanagara Empire, is situated on its banks
It flows through the Nilgiris and creates the Shivanasamudra Falls
It is where Nagarjuna Sagar Dam — the world's largest masonry dam — is built
questions.explanation: Hampi (Karnataka) — the ruined capital of the Vijayanagara Empire — is situated on the banks of the Tungabhadra River. Hampi is a UNESCO World Heritage Site with spectacular boulder-strewn landscapes and over 1,600 monuments. The Tungabhadra is formed by the joining of the Tunga and Bhadra rivers in Chikmagalur district (Karnataka). The Tungabhadra Dam near Hospet (Karnataka) irrigates much of northern Karnataka. Nagarjuna Sagar is on the Krishna itself (not on Tungabhadra).

questions.text: Which of the following rivers does NOT flow into the Bay of Bengal?
questions.explanation: Tapti (Tapi) flows west into the Arabian Sea (via Gulf of Khambhat near Surat). It is one of the two major west-flowing peninsular rivers (Narmada and Tapti), flowing through rift valleys. Mahanadi, Godavari, and Cauvery are all east-flowing rivers that drain into the Bay of Bengal. In SSC exams, the 'odd one out' pattern frequently tests this — identify the west-flowing river among east-flowing ones, or vice versa.

questions.text: The Sardar Sarovar Dam, which was at the centre of the Narmada Bachao Andolan controversy, is located in which state?
questions.explanation: Sardar Sarovar Dam is located in Kevadia, Gujarat (on the Narmada River). While the Narmada originates in Madhya Pradesh and flows through Maharashtra and Gujarat, the main dam is in Gujarat. The Narmada Bachao Andolan (Save Narmada Movement) — led by Medha Patkar and Baba Amte — protested the displacement of hundreds of thousands of tribal people in MP, Maharashtra, and Gujarat due to the rising reservoir behind the dam. The Supreme Court eventually allowed construction to proceed in phases.

questions.text: The 'Granary of South India' refers to the delta of which river?
questions.explanation: The Cauvery delta (in the Thanjavur region of Tamil Nadu) is called the 'Granary of South India' because of its extraordinary agricultural productivity — particularly for rice. The fertile alluvial soil, reliable irrigation from the Cauvery, and a long history of irrigation engineering (the Grand Anicut / Kallanai Dam built by Karikala Chola ~2,000 years ago is among the world's oldest water diversion structures, still in use). The Godavari-Krishna delta is the 'Granary of Andhra Pradesh'; Chhattisgarh plain is the 'Rice Bowl of Central India'.

questions.text: Arrange these east-flowing peninsular rivers in order from north to south as they reach the Bay of Bengal coast:
questions.explanation: Moving down India's east coast from north to south, the major east-flowing peninsular rivers reach the sea in this order: Mahanadi (Odisha) → Godavari (Andhra Pradesh) → Krishna (Andhra Pradesh) → Cauvery (Tamil Nadu). Memory trick: MaGiK-C (Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, Cauvery). This sequence question is a regular feature in SSC CGL and CHSL exams — both forward and reverse ordering is tested.
